title: The Village That Began Everything
subtitle: At the Foot of the Bolan Pass, a Village Learns to Shape Time
era: Neolithic and Chalcolithic Mehrgarh, c. 7000-3300 BCE
region: Mehrgarh, Kachi Plain, Balochistan, at the foot of the Bolan Pass
moral: Great cities begin with patient hands in small villages.

sources.citation: J.-F. Jarrige et al., Mehrgarh: Field Reports 1975-1985 (1995)
sources.note: Primary excavation record for settlement, burials, craft and radiocarbon sequence.

sources.citation: L. Costantini, 'The Beginning of Agriculture in the Kachi Plain' in South Asian Archaeology 1981 (1984)
sources.note: Evidence for early crops and cotton at Mehrgarh.

sources.citation: R. Macchiarelli et al., 'The Earliest Evidence of Dental Drilling in Mehrgarh, Pakistan,' Nature 440 (2006)

panels.shot: wide, low-angle vista of village and plain

dialogue.text: At Mehrgarh, beside the Bolan Pass, early farmers of South Asia begin a long journey from village hearths to the great cities of the Indus.
Seven thousand years before the Common Era, the Kachi Plain lies between high stone hills and thirsty dust, a doorway from the Iranian plateau into the Indian subcontinent.
Here rises Mehrgarh, not yet a city, but a tight weave of houses and courtyards where people choose to stay, season after season, instead of endlessly following wild herds.
In Mehrgarh’s first days there is no pottery, no metal, only mud-brick, stone, and memory. Yet wheat and barley seeds fall into furrows, tying families to earth and season.
Sheep and goats, once only hunted, now sleep behind village walls. Cattle graze close, heavy and patient. Herds are no longer strangers on the horizon, but wealth that breathes at the doorstep.
Rooms are small and dark, but they hold something new: surplus. Grain in baskets, tools on walls, tools for tomorrow’s work, not only today’s hunt.
Farming is hard, slow work. Wood and stone cut the soil. Hands scatter seed. The promise is simple and immense: if they endure the wait, the earth will give more than it took.
With farming comes rhythm. Sowing, tending, harvest, fallow. Nights are no longer spent beneath strange skies, but under the same horizon, year after year.

panels.shot: close-up of early burial pit from above

dialogue.text: The dead are no longer left to the wild alone. They rest near the homes they helped raise, with beads and tools for a journey none can see, but all respect.
Generations pass. Clay, once only walls and floors, becomes vessels. Beads of stone and shell carry stories of rivers and shores far from the Bolan Pass.

panels.shot: close-up on bead and copper speck

dialogue.text: In later days, a new color glints among the dust: native copper, hammered and shaped. Soft metal joins stone and clay, hinting at powers still unborn.
From plants that once only scratched at bare feet, they tease out delicate fibers. Early cotton threads twist under careful fingers, clothing the body and the idea of comfort.

panels.shot: interior close-up on patient and healer

dialogue.text: But with grain comes a hidden cost. Teeth grind seeds and stone grit day after day, aching and cracking. Pain digs into jaws and refuses to leave.

panels.shot: extreme close-up on tooth and tool

dialogue.text: In Mehrgarh, some risk a daring cure. With tiny stone drills they pierce diseased molars, seeking to release pain itself. What began as farming now demands medicine.

dialogue.speaker: mehrgarh_farmers
dialogue.text: We learn, or we suffer. The earth is not the only thing we must master.
Paths over the Bolan Pass carry more than feet. Shells from distant seas, stones from far hills, whispers of other valleys arrive. Mehrgarh becomes a knot in widening trade.
Centuries fold into one another. Houses are built, abandoned, rebuilt. Pottery grows finer, copper tools more common. Mehrgarh reshapes itself again and again, yet the ground remembers every wall.

panels.shot: wide shot fading toward Indus plains

dialogue.text: To the east, beyond sight, lie rivers that will one day carry brick cities and granaries. Mehrgarh’s people never see those skylines, yet their choices prepare the road.

panels.shot: slow push-in on farmer-healer’s thoughtful face

dialogue.text: Mehrgarh will not become Harappa or Mohenjo-daro, yet its quiet revolutions in seed, herd, craft and healing echo through millennia. From such patient villages, the Indus civilisation learns to dream in brick and city.
